svg.title: NPU stack responsibility map
svg.desc: A structural diagram showing all 7 layers of the stack from PyTorch at the top to FPGA fabric at the bottom, color-coded by who is responsible for each layer.

g.text: Layer 1 — PyTorch / TorchDynamo
Tool: PyTorch upstream + torch.compile(backend="openxla")
Handles: bytecode intercept, FX graph extraction, reuse on repeated calls
You do: nothing — user just writes normal PyTorch code
Layer 2 — PyTorch/XLA LazyTensor bridge
Tool: torch_xla C++ library (pip install torch_xla)
Handles: ATen op → XLA IR lowering, lazy graph accumulation, sync triggers
You do: install torch_xla, register your PJRT plugin as the xla device
Layer 3 — XLA compiler (StableHLO → optimised HLO)
Tool: OpenXLA (libxla, libstablehlo — linked into your PJRT plugin)
Handles: CSE, op fusion, constant folding, buffer allocation, shape inference
You do: link against libxla; optionally dump HLO with XLA_FLAGS for debugging
Layer 4a — PJRT plugin: compiler (HLO → NPU ISA)
Tool: OpenXLA PJRT C API headers + your C++ code
You build: HLO graph walker, op classifier, GEMM tiler (32×32 tiles),
FP8 weight packer, NPU ISA binary emitter, CPU fallback splitter
Configure: tile size, FP8 format (E4M3 vs E5M2), accumulator dtype (FP32)
Layer 4b — PJRT plugin: runtime (execute + buffer mgmt)
Tool: your C++ code calling Linux driver via mmap / ioctl
You build: cache flush/invalidate calls, AXI-Lite register writes,
DMA setup, poll/interrupt wait, output buffer handoff to PyTorch
Layer 5 — Linux kernel driver (UIO / platform driver)
Tool: PetaLinux / Ubuntu 22.04 on KR260 + Xilinx AXI DMA kernel driver (built-in)
You build: UIO module (~250 lines C): mmap NPU regs, dma_alloc_coherent,
interrupt wake-up; device tree overlay (.dtso) for NPU IP node
Layer 6 — AXI interconnect (PS ↔ PL)
Tool: Vivado IP integrator — Xilinx AXI DMA v7.1, AXI Interconnect, AXI4-Stream FIFO IPs
You configure: enable HP0/HP1 FPD ports on PS block; connect GP0 to NPU AXI-Lite slave;
set DMA data width (128-bit), disable scatter-gather for simplicity;
assign address map; connect interrupts to PS interrupt controller
Layer 7 — NPU hardware (FPGA PL fabric)
Tool: Vivado RTL synthesis + place-and-route; DSP48E2, BRAM36 primitives used automatically
You build: PE (FP8 MAC), systolic array controller + skew buffers, Weight Buffer,
Activation Buffer (double-buffered), Accumulator Buffer, VPU (ReLU/GELU/LN),
AXI4-Stream ↔ SRAM bridge, AXI-Lite control registers, Instruction Sequencer
Configure: clock frequency target, PBLOCK floorplan constraints (XDC), pipeline depth
Must not forget: tile zero-padding, TLAST assertion, FP8 re-quantization scaling
